reviewSummary: All four specialist reviews pass the supplied task. Within the declared single-process synthetic demonstration scope, the evidence supports all eight acceptance criteria: expiring single-use tokens, neutral account-independent responses, deferred account work, identifier-safe telemetry, deterministic redemption behavior, failure isolation, and automated coverage. The final artifacts are hash-bound to clean scoped commit c06fcf33d345747eb2b1df820ec274619cf25ec4 with a passing post-commit test. Readiness does not extend to production durability, multi-process atomicity, deployment, or durable audit recording.
narrowClaim: The covered evidence has not changed since it was sealed by the holder of the corresponding signing key.

residualFindings.title: Production queue and multi-process atomicity remain outside demonstrated coverage
Audit delivery is best-effort rather than durable
Multi-process durability and atomicity are not demonstrated
Audit delivery is safe but best-effort

limitations: Atomic redemption is guaranteed only within one JavaScript process. Multi-process or restart-safe production use requires a persistent shared store with transactional compare-and-set semantics, as explicitly documented in the committed artifacts.
The scheduler is an injected demonstration boundary rather than a durable production queue; bounded retries, monitoring, and durable failure handling are outside the declared scope.
Audit submission is best-effort and deliberately not awaited, so the implementation proves a safe audit attempt without guaranteeing durable recording when the audit transport stalls or fails.
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, hosted verification, deployment proof, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not claimed by this review.

The token store is intentionally in-memory and coordinates redemption only within one JavaScript process. Multi-process or restart-safe production use requires a persistent shared store with an atomic transaction or compare-and-set operation; this is explicitly outside the demonstration scope.

Token entropy depends on the injected generateToken implementation. The supplied synthetic generator is deterministic for testing and is not evidence of a production cryptographically secure generator.

The scheduler is an injected contract. Production durability, bounded retries, monitoring, rate limiting, abuse controls, delivery-channel security, and deployment configuration were not demonstrated.
Audit submission is best-effort and intentionally does not delay the neutral response. The evidence proves safe invocation and failure isolation, not durable audit persistence.
Repository-wide validation, hosted behavior, deployment controls, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not covered by this verdict.
No dependency vulnerability scan or production authentication and authorization integration was supplied; the reviewed package manifest contains no declared dependencies.
No hosted, deployment, release, repository-wide canonical-validation, or human-acceptance evidence was supplied; these surfaces were explicitly outside the isolated workspace scope.

The post-commit test evidence reports command success but not per-test output; the detailed 16-test output comes from the immediately preceding scoped validation, while artifact hashes and the final Git-state record provide the integrity linkage.
No real email provider, durable scheduler, persistent database, multi-process worker, or restart-recovery integration tests are included; this does not contradict the declared demonstration criteria.

Atomic reservation and token persistence are limited to one JavaScript process and do not survive restart; multi-worker production use requires a shared persistent store with transactional or compare-and-set semantics. This limitation is explicitly documented in the final source and task artifact (ev_final_git_state_c06fcf33d345).

The scheduler behavior is demonstrated with an injected deterministic deferred scheduler. Durability, retries, monitoring, and behavior across production workers are outside the declared scope (ev_final_git_state_c06fcf33d345).
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, hosted verification, deployment proof, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not established by this evidence (ev_000014).

The post-commit test record reports command, commit, exit code, and pass status but not individual test output; individual 16/16 results are supplied by the matching pre-commit validation, while the final-state record binds the artifacts and post-commit result (ev_post_commit_test_c06fcf33d345, ev_000013, ev_final_git_state_c06fcf33d345).
This synthesis is an assessment of supplied evidence and specialist model judgments, not certification or independent execution.
No tools were available to execute tests or inspect artifacts beyond the supplied digest.

The post-commit evidence records command success rather than individual test output; the detailed 16-test result comes from the preceding scoped validation, with final artifact hashes and commit state providing the stated linkage.
Token entropy in production depends on the injected generator; the deterministic synthetic generator is not evidence of a cryptographically secure production generator.

Rate limiting, abuse prevention, real delivery-provider security, production authentication and authorization integration, dependency vulnerability scanning, and deployment configuration were not demonstrated.
Repository-wide canonical validation, release checks, hosted verification, deployment proof, and human acceptance were explicitly unavailable and are not covered by the readiness verdict.
